name: Purine salvage pathway
summary: Cytosolic recycling of preformed purine bases (adenine, hypoxanthine, guanine) and nucleosides back into AMP, IMP and GMP, sparing the ~5 ATP cost of de novo synthesis and centered on the phosphoribosyltransferases APRT and HGPRT.
description:
Cells recover the purine bases and nucleosides released by nucleic-acid and nucleotide turnover instead of degrading them to uric acid. Two phosphoribosyltransferases do most of the work, using 5-phospho-alpha-D-ribose 1-diphosphate (PRPP) as the ribose-phosphate donor: **adenine phosphoribosyltransferase (APRT)** converts adenine to AMP, and **hypoxanthine-guanine phosphoribosyltransferase (HGPRT)** converts hypoxanthine to IMP and guanine to GMP. **Adenosine kinase** salvages the nucleoside adenosine directly to AMP. Salvage is quantitatively dominant over de novo synthesis in most tissues and is essential in cells with little or no de novo capacity — mature erythrocytes, polymorphonuclear leukocytes and, notably, the brain. Complete HGPRT deficiency causes Lesch-Nyhan syndrome (self-mutilation, dystonia, hyperuricemia); partial deficiency causes Kelley-Seegmiller hyperuricemia and gout.

The pathway is fed by a catabolic arm that generates salvageable bases: adenosine deaminase (ADA) and AMP deaminase (AMPD) route adenine nucleosides/nucleotides to inosine and IMP; cytosolic 5'-nucleotidase II (cN-II) dephosphorylates IMP/GMP to their nucleosides; and purine nucleoside phosphorylase (PNP) phosphorolyzes inosine and guanosine to hypoxanthine and guanine plus alpha-D-ribose 1-phosphate. Loss of ADA or PNP causes severe combined immunodeficiency, underscoring how salvage flux governs lymphocyte survival. Guanine deaminase drains guanine to xanthine, committing it to catabolism.

Salvaged IMP is the hub for interconversion. The **adenine branch** — adenylosuccinate synthetase (ADSS2, GTP-dependent) then adenylosuccinate lyase (ADSL) — makes AMP; the **guanine branch** — IMP dehydrogenase (IMPDH2, the rate-limiting, NAD+-dependent committed step) then GMP synthetase (glutamine- and ATP-dependent) — makes GMP. The two branches are reciprocally cross-regulated: GTP powers AMP synthesis while ATP powers GMP synthesis, so an excess of either nucleotide drives production of the other and balances the adenine and guanine pools. Feedback inhibition of IMPDH by GMP and of ADSS by AMP tunes each branch to demand. The AMP-deaminase/ADSS/ADSL triangle also constitutes the **purine nucleotide cycle** (Lowenstein cycle), which in exercising skeletal muscle deaminates AMP to IMP (buffering the adenylate energy charge) and releases fumarate as an anaplerotic input to the TCA cycle. Because proliferating cells depend heavily on nucleotide supply, IMPDH2 is upregulated in many cancers and is the target of the immunosuppressant mycophenolic acid, while activating NT5C2 mutations drive thiopurine resistance in relapsed acute lymphoblastic leukemia.

sources.id: Lehninger Principles of Biochemistry, 8e, Ch. 22 (Biosynthesis of Nucleotides)

references.title: IMP dehydrogenase: structure, mechanism, and inhibition
Crystal structure of human cytosolic 5'-nucleotidase II: insights into allosteric regulation and substrate recognition
Hypothesized deficiency of guanine-based purines may contribute to abnormalities of neurodevelopment ... in Lesch-Nyhan syndrome

compartments.name: Cytosol
compartments.description: Site of the purine salvage and interconversion reactions modeled here.

enzymes.name: Adenine phosphoribosyltransferase (APRT)
enzymes.description: Salvages adenine to AMP using PRPP. Loss-of-function causes 2,8-dihydroxyadenine urolithiasis.

enzymes.name: Hypoxanthine-guanine phosphoribosyltransferase (HGPRT)
enzymes.description: Salvages hypoxanthine to IMP and guanine to GMP. Complete deficiency causes Lesch-Nyhan syndrome; partial deficiency causes Kelley-Seegmiller hyperuricemia.

enzymes.name: Adenosine kinase (ADK)
enzymes.description: Phosphorylates adenosine to AMP; the major route controlling intracellular and extracellular adenosine, and thus adenosine-receptor signaling. Shows substrate inhibition by adenosine.

enzymes.name: Adenosine deaminase (ADA)
enzymes.description: Irreversibly deaminates adenosine (and 2'-deoxyadenosine) to inosine. Deficiency causes adenosine-deaminase severe combined immunodeficiency (ADA-SCID).

enzymes.name: Purine nucleoside phosphorylase (PNP)
enzymes.description: Phosphorolyzes inosine, guanosine and their 2'-deoxy forms to the free base plus alpha-D-ribose(2-deoxyribose) 1-phosphate. Deficiency causes T-cell immunodeficiency.

enzymes.name: Cytosolic purine 5'-nucleotidase II (cN-II)
enzymes.description: Preferentially dephosphorylates IMP, GMP and XMP, controlling the intracellular nucleotide-monophosphate pool. Allosterically activated by ATP, diadenosine polyphosphates and 2,3-bisphosphoglycerate. Activating mutations drive thiopurine resistance in relapsed acute lymphoblastic leukemia.

enzymes.name: AMP deaminase 1 (muscle isoform)
enzymes.description: Deaminates AMP to IMP + ammonia; the entry step of the purine nucleotide cycle. Activated by ATP/ADP, inhibited by Pi and GTP. Isozymes AMPD2 (liver/ubiquitous) and AMPD3 (erythrocyte) serve other tissues. AMPD1 deficiency causes exercise-induced myopathy.

enzymes.name: Guanine deaminase (guanase)
enzymes.description: Deaminates guanine to xanthine, committing guanine to catabolism (urate) rather than salvage.

enzymes.name: Adenylosuccinate synthetase isozyme 2 (non-muscle)
enzymes.description: GTP-dependent, committed step of the IMP-to-AMP branch; condenses IMP with L-aspartate. Muscle isozyme ADSS1 (ADSSL1) supports the purine nucleotide cycle.

enzymes.name: Adenylosuccinate lyase (ADSL)
enzymes.description: Bifunctional: cleaves adenylosuccinate to AMP + fumarate here, and also cleaves SAICAR in de novo purine synthesis. Deficiency causes a neurodevelopmental disorder.

enzymes.name: Inosine-5'-monophosphate dehydrogenase 2 (IMPDH2)
enzymes.description: NAD+-dependent, rate-limiting committed step of guanine-nucleotide synthesis. Feedback-inhibited by GMP; upregulated in proliferating and malignant cells (a MYC target) and inhibited by the immunosuppressant mycophenolic acid.

enzymes.name: GMP synthetase [glutamine-hydrolyzing]
enzymes.description: ATP- and glutamine-dependent amidation of XMP to GMP; the ATP requirement links guanine-nucleotide output to adenylate energy status.

reactions.name: Adenylosuccinate synthesis (ADSS, committed step of adenine branch)
reactions.equation: IMP + L-Aspartate + GTP -> Adenylosuccinate + GDP + Orthophosphate

reactions.name: IMP oxidation to XMP (IMPDH, rate-limiting committed step of guanine branch)
reactions.equation: IMP + NAD+ + H2O -> XMP + NADH + H+

reactions.name: GMP synthesis (GMP synthetase, glutamine-hydrolyzing)
reactions.equation: XMP + L-Glutamine + ATP + H2O -> GMP + L-Glutamate + AMP + Diphosphate

regulations.mechanism: Guanine nucleotides (GMP, and GDP) feedback-inhibit IMPDH, the rate-limiting enzyme committing IMP to guanine-nucleotide synthesis, matching GMP output to demand.
AMP, the end product of the IMP-to-AMP branch, feedback-inhibits adenylosuccinate synthetase, balancing adenine-nucleotide output against the parallel guanine branch.
The purine nucleotide products IMP and GMP inhibit HGPRT (product/feedback inhibition), so a replete nucleotide pool throttles further base salvage.
GMP is a product-inhibitor of HGPRT, contributing to feedback control of guanine salvage.
AMP, the product of adenine salvage, feedback-inhibits APRT, preventing overproduction of adenine nucleotides.
GTP allosterically inhibits AMP deaminase. Because GTP is consumed to make AMP (via ADSS), abundant guanine nucleotides restrain AMP deamination and protect the adenine-nucleotide pool.

ATP and ADP allosterically activate skeletal-muscle AMP deaminase (AMPD1); during vigorous exercise this drives the purine nucleotide cycle, deaminating AMP to IMP to keep the adenylate energy charge high and releasing ammonia and fumarate.

Adenosine kinase exhibits substrate inhibition: at elevated adenosine concentrations the substrate binds a second site and lowers activity, making ADK a sensitive set-point for intracellular and extracellular adenosine and hence purinergic signaling.

Cytosolic 5'-nucleotidase II is allosterically activated by ATP, diadenosine polyphosphates and 2,3-bisphosphoglycerate; effector binding triggers a disorder-to-order transition that organizes the catalytic site, coupling IMP/GMP dephosphorylation to the energy state of the cell.

relations.note: PRPP, the ribose-phosphate donor for every salvage phosphoribosyltransferase, is made by PRPP synthetase from ribose 5-phosphate supplied by the pentose phosphate pathway.
alpha-D-Ribose 1-phosphate released by PNP is isomerized to ribose 5-phosphate (phosphopentomutase), recycling the pentose backbone back toward the pentose phosphate pool and PRPP.

IMP is the branch-point hub shared with de novo purine biosynthesis; salvage regenerates IMP directly from hypoxanthine, bypassing the energetically costly 10-step de novo route, and both feed the shared IMP-to-AMP and IMP-to-GMP branches.

Hypoxanthine not recaptured by HGPRT is oxidized by xanthine oxidase to xanthine and uric acid — the catabolic fate that competes with salvage and, when salvage fails (e.g. HGPRT loss), drives hyperuricemia.
Xanthine (from guanine deamination and from hypoxanthine oxidation) is the committed intermediate of purine catabolism, oxidized to uric acid by xanthine oxidase.

In the purine nucleotide cycle (AMP -> IMP -> adenylosuccinate -> AMP), the ADSL step releases fumarate, an anaplerotic input to the TCA cycle that is quantitatively important in exercising skeletal muscle.
Adenosine is an extracellular signaling molecule acting on P1 (adenosine) receptors; adenosine kinase and adenosine deaminase set its concentration, coupling purine salvage to purinergic signaling.
